justification: Ch3 argues Jesus's titles map onto Hellenistic royal categories. However, the specific mechanism Tabor describes -- ascension requiring a transformed pneumatic body suited to celestial ecology -- is not presented in ch3. The soma pneumatikon concept is new evidence for the existing argument about Hellenistic frameworks for understanding Jesus.

Ch3 inventory 'Son of God was the standard title for Hellenistic rulers' (lines 465-474) with 'Sonship as cosmological-political vocation: ruler embodies heaven's order' directly covers this. The finding frames Son of God as rightful heir of the kingdom, which is the exact argument the chapter makes.

Ch3 argues Kingdom of God was a political institution (lines 691-728) and discusses collective sin/restoration theology. However, the specific framing of kingdom ethics as compassion modeled on divine justice (God sending rain on just and unjust) as a governance requirement is not in the inventory. The love ethic section (lines 871-904) exists but does not present this specific compassion-as-divine-justice governance model from Q context.

Ch3 inventory 'Jesus's birth represented as a new star' (lines 620-633) explicitly includes 'Suetonius (Divus Augustus 94): portents, Senate decree, divine conception, post-mortem appearances -- same elements as Matthew.' The Augustus-Jesus birth narrative parallel is directly covered with the same Suetonius evidence.

Ch3 argues Jesus was framed in imperial political-religious idiom (lines 22-57). However, Adela Collins's specific scholarly observation that Mark's Gospel engages polemically with the Roman Imperial cult is not cited as evidence. Collins's Mark-specific polemic analysis is new evidence for the existing argument.

Matthew's literary construction of Jesus as a new Moses figure belongs in chapter 4 (Gospels as Historically Reliable) which discusses Gospel literary techniques, or chapter 5 which discusses Matthew as the Judean gospel with legal-exegetical character.

Ch3 argues Messiah is an authentic royal title for political rulers (lines 445-464). However, the Babylonian substitute king ritual -- where a criminal is anointed, dressed in purple, crowned, and sacrificed -- is not cited in the chapter's evidence. This specific ancient Near Eastern anointing-sacrifice practice is new evidence for the existing argument.
Jesus's crucifixion as part of Roman imperial violence patterns belongs in chapter 2 which covers crucifixion, Roman political calculation, and execution protocol extensively.

Ch3 argues Son of God was a standard Hellenistic title and God manifested in the flesh continues Hellenistic Epiphanes title (lines 505-512). Simon Magus claiming to be 'the great power' (god incarnate) is a new comparative example not cited in the chapter.

Ch3 covers Alexander's divine sonship (lines 574-580) and birth star motifs (lines 620-633), but does not specifically enumerate 18 connection points between Alexander's birth narrative and Matthew's. The argument exists; the 18 connection points comparison is new evidence.

Ch3 discusses Hellenistic royal titles extensively but does not specifically cite Dionysus's water-to-wine miracle as evidence. Ch4 mentions the Dionysian Gospel. For ch3, the argument exists but the Dionysus water-to-wine evidence is not in ch3's inventory.

Ch3 inventory 'Messiah is the authentic Judean royal title' (lines 445-464) includes Christos as existing Greek word meaning anointed and the Hasmonean anointed kings. The finding that Paul used Christos within 20 years and it means king of the Jews is the same argument and evidence category.
Jesus's political popularity in Jerusalem and Pilate's motivation belongs in chapter 2 which covers Jesus's crucifixion, trial, and political circumstances extensively.

Ch3 inventory includes John the Baptist section (lines 345-421) with 'Josephus (Ant. 18.116-119): Antipas executed John to prevent rebellion' and 'Jesus's call to repent as summons to national realignment' (lines 807-822). The finding that kingdom preaching was politically destabilizing is covered.

Ch3 does not have a dedicated section on apotheosis as the Greco-Roman framework for resurrection. Crossan's distinction between apotheosis (Greco-Roman expectation) and Jewish resurrection, with examples of Romulus and Moses, is new evidence for the existing argument about Hellenistic frameworks.

Ch3 inventory argues God manifested in the flesh continues Hellenistic Epiphanes title (lines 505-512) and Son of God was standard title (lines 465-474). The finding that the god-man concept is Greek/pagan rather than Jewish restates the chapter's argument.

Ch3 covers Josephus proclaiming Vespasian as ruler foretold in Jewish prophecy (lines 445-464). The finding about both Jesus and Josephus interpreting messianic prophecy through Hellenistic political categories is covered.

Ch3 argues Son of God was a Hellenistic title (lines 465-474). The specific parallel between Acts proclaiming Jesus as Son of God and Pentheus refusing to acknowledge Dionysus as Son of Zeus in the Bacchae is not in ch3's evidence. This is new evidence for the existing argument.

Ch3 argues Son of God is a Hellenistic title (lines 465-474) and discusses Ps 2:7 at resurrection (Acts 13:33). However, the specific framing of Romans 1:4 as apotheosis -- Jesus becoming deified at resurrection paralleling Romulus becoming a star -- is not in the chapter.

Ch3 does not compare Paul's pneumatic resurrection body with Luke/John's physical resurrection appearances. The contrast between Paul's spiritual body and Gospel physicality is new evidence not present in ch3.

Ch3 opens with the argument that the Christian movement spoke Greek and framed Jesus in Hellenistic political-religious idiom (lines 1-57). Global Q&A 'Eastern Mediterranean synthesis' also covers this. The claim about Hellenism permeating even Essenes is a restatement of the book's core thesis.

Ch3 inventory 'Early Christian apologists explicitly argued that Jesus's divine sonship used the same categories as Greek divine sonship' (lines 47-50) includes Justin Martyr, First Apology 21-22 citing Heracles, Asclepius, Dionysus, Perseus. This is exactly the same evidence.

Ch3 cites Justin Martyr and Theophilus of Antioch (lines 47-50). Arnobius of Sicca (~300 CE) making the same argument about Romulus is a new patristic source not cited in the chapter. The argument is the same; Arnobius is new evidence.

Ch3 extensively covers this: Augustus as divi filius (lines 22-57), coins equating Caesar with God (lines 610-619). Crossan's point that Jesus and Caesar are competing divinities is the core argument of ch3.

Ch3 does not argue why the disciples chose resurrection over ascension. Crossan's argument -- that ascension was the standard Greco-Roman expectation accepted by everyone including Jews, and choosing resurrection was a deliberate Pharisee-sectarian reframing -- is a new argument with new evidence.
Ch3 covers Augustus as divi filius (lines 22-57) and coins equating Caesar with God (lines 610-619). The finding that Jesus is propaganda against Roman Imperial cult is the chapter's core argument.

Global Q&A explicitly rejects the mystery cult framing. Christianity was not a mystery cult but a continuation of the old imperial cult under foreign rule. The finding's mystery cult conclusion contradicts the book's framework.

Ch3 discusses divine conception through Plutarch/Luke (lines 476-485). Plutarch's Isis and Osiris as source for virgin birth motifs entering Gospels is new evidence. While the mystery cult framing is rejected per global Q&A, the evidence about narrative patterns is valid.

Ch3 discusses birth narratives and Son of God. Luke's specific use of swaddling clothes motif from Greek myths about divine children (Ion son of Apollo, Hermes son of Zeus, Heracles son of Zeus) is not in the chapter's evidence.

Ch3 covers euangelion as political proclamation language and Augustus's titles (lines 22-57, 610-619). Ch2 inventory covers euangelion as political term. Luke's savior/soter/peace language paralleling Augustus is the same argument and evidence type.

Ch3 does not mention the January 6 Epiphany date transfer from Dionysus to Jesus or Epiphanius of Salamis's attestation. This is new evidence for the existing argument about Hellenistic religious frameworks shaping Christianity.

Ch3 inventory (lines 47-50) includes Justin Martyr citing Asclepius and 'Via Latina catacomb: Heracles raising Alcestis beside Jesus raising Lazarus.' The Jesus-Asclepius healer parallel and Lazarus/Alcestis parallel are directly covered.

Ch3 discusses Ps 2:7 as investiture formula (lines 51-53). Walsh's specific mechanism -- pneuma as the substance that turns Jesus into God's son and then transforms gentiles into adopted children/co-heirs -- is new evidence for the existing argument about Son of God as conferred status.

Ch3 discusses kingdom theology. The Stoic cosmological framework underlying Paul's eschatology -- Christ's divine army vs archons of the age as earthly rulers, Stoic physics of intermediate divine beings below the moon -- is not in ch3's evidence.
Paul's authority claims and divination theology belongs in chapter 5 (Pauline Epistles) which covers Paul's political profile and legitimacy mechanisms.

Ch3 covers Augustus as divi filius (lines 22-57). The emperor's genius -- an eternal divine being worshipped at altars even while the emperor lives -- is a distinct theological mechanism (living divine self) not in the chapter.
Ch3 inventory (lines 610-619) includes coins with Augustus Caesar as god. The finding that Son of God and Savior appeared on Roman coins is the same evidence.
Ch3 inventory (lines 620-633) includes Augustus's deification of Julius Caesar. Ehrman's point about using a comet is the same evidence.
Mark's passion narrative paralleling Homer's Iliad belongs in chapter 4 (Gospel literary composition).
Temple curtain parallel to fall of Troy belongs in chapter 4 (Gospel literary composition).

Ch3 inventory covers Alexander's divine sonship (lines 574-580) and birth star motifs (lines 620-633). The Matthew-Alexander birth narrative parallel with specific elements is the same evidence category.

Ch3 cites apologist comparisons (lines 47-50) but does not use the specific translation fable framework or the 12 of 15 sub-themes analysis. This systematic framework is new evidence for the existing argument.

Ch3 covers Augustus's deification (lines 610-633). The specific institutional mechanisms -- military declaring divine sightings, financial incentives for eyewitness testimony, missing bodies as proof -- are not detailed in the chapter.

Ch3 covers Basileia tou Theou (lines 691-728) and NT evidence of earthly sovereignty (lines 697-728). Ch2 covers ekklesia as civic assembly. Ch5 covers ekklesia extensively. The finding's point is covered across chapters.
Jesus's use of Greek vocabulary belongs in chapter 2 which covers Jesus's education and Hellenistic cultural context.

Ch3 argues Jesus's titles competed with Hellenistic divine claims (lines 22-57). The archaeological observation of Caesarea Philippi with temples to Augustus and Pan where Jesus asked his identity question is not in ch3's evidence.

Ch3 discusses the Father in Greek philosophical categories (lines 97-126) and Logos (lines 486-504). Gmirkin's argument that monotheism itself was a Greek philosophical innovation is not stated as such in the chapter.

Ch3 inventory (lines 445-464) includes Josephus proclaiming Vespasian as ruler foretold in Jewish prophecy and Suetonius/Tacitus confirmations. The finding about Flavians claiming Jewish messianic titles is the same evidence.
Ch3 cites individual apologist examples (lines 47-50) but does not cite the statistical count of 79 translation fables with 56+ vanished bodies. This quantitative evidence is new.
Luke's Emmaus Road using Homeric patterns belongs in chapter 4 or chapter 5.
Ch3 inventory 'Kyrios was the standard imperial honorific' (lines 553-562) directly covers the argument that Kyrios applied to Jesus is Greco-Roman language.
Paul's reversal of Dead Sea Scroll messianic language belongs in chapter 5 (Pauline Epistles).

Ch3 has a full section on the love ethic as shrewd political strategy under occupation (lines 871-904). The book already argues that "love your enemy" has a pro-Roman surface appearance — that IS the strategy. Eisenman observing that it looks pro-Roman is exactly what the book explains and contextualizes. Not a contradiction; the book already addresses this.
Ch3 inventory 'Son of Father' (lines 476-485) includes Plutarch/Plato dynamis and pneuma and Luke 1:35 using same vocabulary. This is exactly the same evidence.
Ch3 discusses Epiphanes (lines 505-512) but does not analyze the Transfiguration as following classical Greco-Egyptian epiphany structure. This specific narrative-structural analysis is new evidence.

Ch3 discusses Soter and Euergetes titles (lines 546-587). The principle that benefaction/miracles served as the primary proof of divinity across cultures -- as a formal mechanism -- is not articulated as such in the chapter.

Ch3 covers Kyrios (lines 553-562) and Philippians 2:11. However, the parallel to pharaohs receiving five divine names at coronation and rulers receiving divine names at deification is not in the chapter.

Ch3 mentions Heracles in Justin Martyr's comparison (lines 47-50). The specific concept of corporeal immortalization -- flesh dissolving, immortal body raised -- as distinct from simple resurrection is new detailed evidence.
Ch3 inventory (lines 47-50) includes Theophilus citing Heracles ascending after the pyre and Asclepius struck by thunderbolt. Romulus is implied. The finding repeats covered evidence.
Ch3 covers Philo's Logos and Moses at death (lines 486-504, 620-633). Horace's portrayal of Augustus as permanent incarnation of Mercury/Hermes is new evidence not in the chapter.
Ch3 extensively covers Augustus as divi filius (lines 22-57) and comparative titulature (lines 610-619). Crossan's point that every title Caesar gets, Jesus gets, is the chapter's core argument.
Ch3 inventory (lines 610-619) includes coins with theos sebastos kaiser and Render unto Caesar exchange. This is the exact same evidence.
Ch3 inventory (lines 807-822) and kingdom sections (lines 691-728) cover the argument that proclaiming God's rule under Roman occupation was politically dangerous.

Ch3 argues kingdom was a political institution (lines 691-728). However, the specific argument that resurrection theology was consciously used as a recruiting tool to maintain movement continuity after the leader's death is a distinct argument not made in ch3. The kingdom evidence exists; the resurrection-as-recruiting-strategy argument is new.

Ch3 covers Kyrios as imperial honorific (lines 553-562). The observation that the liturgical phrase kyrie eleison derives from imperial language for addressing the emperor is new evidence for the existing argument.

Ch3 covers Messiah as authentic Judean royal title (lines 445-464) including King of the Jews as real royal title and charge for execution. The finding restates this with Josephus on messianic claimants.

Ch3 argues Jesus was framed in imperial political-religious idiom. Collins's specific analysis of Mark being influenced by the Roman imperial cult is a new scholarly observation not cited in the chapter.
Ch3 inventory (lines 22-57) covers Augustus's titles and comparison to Jesus's. The finding restates covered evidence.

Ch3 cites individual apologist examples (lines 47-50). The chronological claim of dying and rising god motifs from 8th century BCE through Jesus's time provides temporal breadth not quantified in the chapter.

Ch3 discusses Ps 2:7 at baptism (lines 51-53). MacDonald's philological observation that agapas (beloved) in 'you are my beloved Son' does not appear in Psalm 2 but is pervasive in Homer for royal/divine favorites is significant new evidence not in the chapter.

Ch3 argues Basileia tou Theou is a political institution (lines 691-728). The parallel between Jesus declaring the kingdom of his father and Telemachus claiming Odysseus's kingdom of Ithaca is a new literary parallel not in the chapter.

Ch3 mentions Serapis in the Ptolemaic section (lines 581-587). However, the specific enumeration of Serapis's parallel attributes to Jesus -- born of mortal and god, healer, resurrects dead, apotheosis -- is not detailed in the chapter.
Ch3 inventory (lines 620-633) includes Suetonius on Augustus's portents and deification. Augustus as son of Apollo with staged apotheosis and eyewitnesses is the same evidence.
Gospel mimesis of Homer as literary composition technique belongs in chapter 4 rather than chapter 3.

Ch3 and ch2 discuss Christos as Greek royal title. Zinner's argument that Josephus used Christ as a personal name (not messianic title) for his Greek audience, paralleling how Buddha shifted from title to name, is new evidence for how Christos functioned in Hellenistic context.
The Testimonium Flavianum's tone and audience context belongs in chapter 2 which covers the Testimonium extensively.

Ch3 covers Alexander's divine sonship (lines 574-580), Augustus as divi filius (lines 22-57), Heracles in Justin Martyr (lines 47-50), and divine conception without sexual union (lines 476-485). Singer's point restates ch3's core argument.

Ch3 discusses Ps 2:7 at baptism (lines 51-53) and John's Logos (lines 486-504). The systematic tracing of theological progression -- Mark (adoption at baptism), Matthew/Luke (pre-natal), John (pre-eternal) -- as increasing Greek metaphysical framing is not presented as a single argument in the chapter.
Eucharist origins belong in chapter 4 or chapter 5.

Ch3 covers apologist comparisons (lines 47-50). Price's observation about Hellenistic novels where crucified heroes survive -- at least three such novels -- is a different literary genre comparison not in the chapter.
Earlier passion narratives with Jesus surviving crucifixion belongs in chapter 2.

Ch3 inventory covers titles as pre-existing political terms (lines 22-57) and Suetonius parallels (lines 620-633). Gospel writers employing Greco-Roman conventions to portray Jesus as King figure is the chapter's core argument.

Ch3 mentions Dionysus in Justin Martyr's comparison (lines 47-50). The specific enumeration of Jesus as adaptation of Dionysus with parallel titles, birth narratives, and 'I am the true vine' as clear imitations goes beyond what the chapter presents.
Ch3 inventory (lines 486-504) includes Philo's Logos as firstborn son of God. The finding about Philo's pre-Gospel theology including Logos, Son of God, and divine power is directly covered.
Jason's gymnasium in Jerusalem demonstrating Greek infrastructure belongs in chapter 2.

Ch3 argues the Christian movement spoke Greek (lines 1-57). Miller's criticism that Biblical Greek is an artificial category and texts should be treated as continuous with Greek usage is a new methodological argument supporting the existing framework.
Ch3 inventory (lines 47-50) includes Justin Martyr citing sons of Zeus. The finding restates the same evidence.

Ch3 mentions apologist comparisons (lines 47-50). The detailed enumeration of Romulus-Jesus parallels -- missing bodies, prodigies, darkness, ascensions, Son of God titles, road appearances, cloud ascension, deification -- goes well beyond what ch3 presents.
The archaeological context of Caesarea Philippi with temples to Augustus and Pan where Jesus asks his identity question is not in ch3's evidence. New evidence for competing Hellenistic divine claims.
Gospel mimesis of Homer belongs in chapter 4.

Ch3 discusses Ps 2:7 at baptism (lines 51-53). The Gnostic adoption theology -- Jesus became Son of God at baptism through the Spirit/Dove rather than from birth -- is new evidence (Gnostic adoptionist tradition) for the existing argument about Son of God as conferred status.

Ch3 discusses 4Q246 (line 46) and dual messiah framework (lines 345-421). The Teacher of Righteousness appropriating messianic titles from Isaiah's servant songs and applying royal language to himself is new evidence showing pre-Christian appropriation of royal messianic titulature.

Ch3 discusses Ps 2:7 at resurrection as investiture (Acts 13:33, lines 51-53). The specific framing of resurrection as apotheosis -- exaltation to heavenly throne among Elohim, distinct from bodily reanimation -- is new evidence.
Ch3 inventory (lines 345-421) includes Dead Sea Scrolls dual messiah (Community Rule 1QS IX:11) and Damascus Document (CD XIX-XX). The two-messiah framework is directly covered.
Mimesis/syncrisis as literary techniques belong in chapter 4.

Ch3 discusses the star motif in Hellenistic royal tradition (lines 620-633). The finding adds that Jewish messianic tradition specifically used a star symbol and early Christians abandoned this Jewish imagery for Greco-Roman political symbols. This deliberate abandonment is new evidence.
Ch3 inventory (lines 486-504) covers the Logos concept from Heraclitus, Stoics, and Middle Platonists before Philo. The finding about pre-Socratic Nous is the same argument and evidence category.

Ch3 does not use the term apotheosis or distinguish it from metamorphosis (for the living) in describing Paul's resurrection understanding. Tabor's specific Greek terminological precision and application to Paul is new evidence.
Ch3 covers Alexander's divine sonship (lines 574-580) and Augustus as divi filius (lines 22-57). The finding restates the chapter's core argument.
Dionysian mystery practices as origin of Eucharistic practices belongs in chapter 4.
Ch3 inventory (lines 47-50) includes Dionysus among sons of Zeus. The finding that Dionysus was called Son of God is covered by this evidence.

Ch3 discusses only-begotten as dynastic succession marker (lines 476-485). The specific parallel that Dionysus is also only begotten by Zeus -- with Zeus giving birth from his side -- is not in the chapter's evidence.
Luke's trial incorporating Socratic trial elements belongs in chapter 4 or chapter 5.
Ch3 includes Dionysus among sons of Zeus (lines 47-50). The finding that Dionysus and Jesus are both son of god is covered. The wine connection is more relevant to ch4's Dionysian Gospel analysis.
Ch3 covers NT evidence of earthly sovereignty (lines 697-728) and kingdom as political institution. Twelve thrones judging twelve tribes is an instance of this political kingdom argument.

Ch3 covers apologist comparisons (lines 47-50) but does not systematically frame apotheosis/deification as the Hellenistic pattern for understanding resurrection. This systematic framework is new evidence.

Ch3 discusses only-begotten (lines 476-485). The observation that Israelite and Phoenician firstborn child sacrifice vocabulary (only begotten, beloved, firstborn son) still dominates Jesus's descriptive language is new cross-cultural evidence.
Ch3 does not discuss the rider of the clouds motif shared across Marduk, Baal, Zeus, Indra, or Daniel 7's revolutionary application to a messianic figure. This is new evidence.

Ch3 does not discuss the political-theological function of storm/cloud-riding deities (Marduk justifying Babylon, Zeus establishing Olympian order). This is new evidence for Hellenistic royal theology.

Ch3 discusses Gnostics denying Father was YHWH (lines 119-126). Pagels's contrast -- claiming divinity as blasphemy (Jewish) vs universal capacity (Thomas tradition) -- is new evidence for the argument about Jewish vs Hellenistic categories.

Ch3 opens with the Hellenistic framework (lines 1-57). Global Q&A covers the Eastern Mediterranean synthesis. Litwa's point about Jews/Christians being enmeshed in the oikoumene is a restatement of the book's framework.
Ch3 inventory (lines 476-485) includes Plutarch on Plato's conception with dynamis and pneuma, and divine conception without sexual union. The finding restates this evidence.

Ch3 covers Philo's Logos as firstborn son of God (lines 486-504). However, the complete enumeration of ALL attributes Philo's archangel shares with Jesus -- firstborn son, agent of creation, high priest, image of God, Lord, intermediary, agent of atonement -- with all except two already in Paul, is more comprehensive than what ch3 presents.
Ch3 inventory (lines 486-504) covers the Logos concept from Heraclitus, Stoics, Middle Platonists before Philo. The finding that Logos pervaded both pagan and Jewish theology is the same argument.

Ch3 covers Logos from Philo to John (lines 486-504). The specific claim that early Christians sold Jesus as the incarnation of Philo's angel -- pre-existent, first created, counterpart to Adam -- adds the incarnational angel mechanism not stated in ch3.
Ch3 discusses divine conception terminology (lines 476-485). The Greek theological distinction between ginomai (made) and genesis (born) from Plutarch is a philological detail not in the chapter.
The systematic translation fable framework with missing bodies, post-mortem appearances, ascension imagery as common topos is new evidence. Ch3 does not cite this systematic analysis.

Ch3 discusses birth narratives (lines 620-633). The identification of Revelation 12's nativity as based on the Zeus/Cronus myth -- Cronus eating sons, paralleling Herod's infanticide -- is new evidence not in the chapter.
Ch3 inventory (lines 620-633) includes Augustus's deification through the Julian star. The finding restates this evidence.

Ch3 does not discuss Virgil's Aeneid as model for the nativity or Faltonia Betitia Proba rewroting the Gospel using only Virgil quotations. This is both new argument and new evidence not in any chapter inventory.
Ch3 inventory (lines 486-504) includes Philo's Logos as firstborn son of God. The finding enumerates specific Pauline parallels but the core argument is covered.

Ch3 mentions Dionysus in apologist comparisons (lines 47-50). The specific framing of Dionysus as Zeus on Earth -- not identical but with Orphic material saying one and the same -- paralleling how Christians frame Jesus-God relation is new comparative theological evidence.

Ch3 mentions Epiphanes title (lines 505-512) but does not analyze the Transfiguration as paralleling Greek epiphany scenes like Demeter in the Hymn to Demeter. This specific literary parallel is new evidence.

Ch3 discusses Father vs Kyrios distinction (lines 288-322). The framework of divine name being invested into people giving them God's authority without being YHWH is a new articulation of authorization language.

Ch3 does not discuss sons of Zebedee as Castor and Pollux (sons of Zeus), or Boanerges as sons of the thunderer Zeus. This is both a new argument (inner circle identified with Greek divine mythology) and new evidence. Not in any chapter inventory.

Ch3 discusses leadership through John the Baptist (lines 345-421). Price's framing of Peter, James, and John as cosmic mediators with supernatural significance is new evidence for Hellenistic royal and cosmic theology.

Ch3 discusses Christos (lines 445-464). Price's argument that anointing derives from Osiris mythology -- anointing with oil resurrects the dead -- is a new evidence claim for the existing argument about Christ as Hellenistic designation.
Global Q&A explicitly rejects the mystery cult framing. Price's claim that Jesus was a savior god of a mystery religion directly contradicts the book's framework.

Ch3 discusses Christos (lines 445-464) as Greek royal title. Price's argument that Christ became a proper name early on and Christians redefined Messiah to fit healer stories is a different argument from ch3's position that Messiah was always a political-royal title. The evidence (Christos usage) exists; the argument is different.
Ch3 inventory (lines 620-633) includes Suetonius on Augustus's portents, which exemplifies imperial panegyric conventions. Matthew using rhetorical handbook conventions is the same argument.

Ch3 discusses Mary's royal imagery (lines 640-690) and kingdom (lines 691-728). MacDonald's argument that the Magnificat and Benedictus are political documents expressing liberation theology is not stated in the chapter.
John's Dionysian imagery belongs in chapter 4 or chapter 5.
Ch3 inventory (lines 476-485) includes Plutarch/Plato with pneuma. Litwa's point about Plutarch and pneuma is the same evidence.
Ch3 discusses Jesus's titles in competition with imperial claims (lines 22-57). Mark's Messianic secret as deliberate inversion of imperial announcement practices is new evidence.
Jesus as Dionysian character in John and Acts paralleling the Bacchae belongs in chapter 4 or chapter 5.
Ch3 discusses titles mapping onto successor kingdoms (lines 422-444). The emphasis on understanding Son of Man through Septuagint Greek origins is new linguistic evidence.
Ch3 discusses Ps 2:7 as investiture (lines 51-53). The Q document's specific account of Jesus being designated/adopted as son of god is new evidence (Q source attestation).
Ch3 inventory (lines 486-504) includes Philo's Logos theology and John's Gospel mirroring it. The finding restates this.
Ch3 does not draw the parallel between logos in the bosom of the father (John 1:18) and Dionysus in Zeus's bosom. This is both new argument and new evidence.
Ch3 does not discuss the Trinity's relationship to the Neoplatonic triad (One/Nous/Psyche). The argument that the Trinity is an adaptation forced into monotheism is new argument and new evidence.
Syncrisis as rhetorical technique belongs in chapter 4.
Mimesis as competitive mythology belongs in chapter 4.
Mimesis as sociological mechanism belongs in chapter 4.

Ch3 cites apologist comparisons (lines 47-50). Lucian's account of Peregrinus showing apotheosis mechanism in real-time -- earthquakes, vultures, instant temple/priesthood -- is new evidence not in the chapter.
Ch3 argues Son of God is Hellenistic title (lines 465-474). Peregrinus elevated to Son of Zeus with temples is a real-time demonstration not in the chapter.
Ch3 does not discuss public spectacular death as culturally valorized Greco-Roman practice. This provides new context for understanding crucifixion within Hellenistic frameworks.
Ch3 inventory covers Basileia tou Theou (lines 691-728) and Ps 2:7 as investiture (lines 51-53). Jesus crowned as prince and declaring kingdom arrived is the chapter's argument.
Ch3 inventory (lines 47-50) includes Dionysus among sons of Zeus. MacDonald's Bacchae quote is the textual basis for Justin Martyr's citation. Already covered.
Ch3 discusses Philo's Logos (lines 486-504). Philo specifically reframing Torah as supreme expression of Stoic natural law is new evidence for Greek philosophical frameworks shaping Jewish theology.
Ch3 inventory (lines 505-512) includes Antiochus IV Epiphanes and Ptolemy V Epiphanes. The finding about epiphany as Hellenistic royal title is the same evidence.
Ch3 discusses Ps 2:7 at baptism (lines 51-53). Basilides' understanding of baptism as the epiphany/incarnation point is new historical evidence (Basilides tradition) for the existing argument.
Mark's engagement with Middle Platonic philosophy belongs in chapter 4 or chapter 5.
Gospel authorship within Greco-Roman literary traditions belongs in chapter 4 or chapter 5.
Acts employing syncrisis belongs in chapter 4 or chapter 5.

Ch3 mentions apologist comparisons (lines 47-50). Romulus's specific ascension narrative -- vanishing before troops, vision to senator, declared a god -- is new detailed evidence for the existing argument.
Mark's opening word arche as founding myth declaration belongs in chapter 4.
Gerasene demoniac Homeric parallels belong in chapter 4.
Matthew removing Homeric parallels belongs in chapter 4.
Ch3 inventory (lines 47-50) includes Justin Martyr, First Apology 21-22. This is the exact same evidence.
Ch3 inventory (lines 47-50) includes Theophilus citing Heracles and Asclepius. This is the exact same evidence.

Ch3 discusses only-begotten (lines 476-485). Pagels's observation that John's insistence on only begotten was polemical response to Thomas's teaching that all are children of God is new evidence about intra-Christian competition over divine sonship terminology.
Ch3 discusses Christ as Helios and Good Shepherd as Hermes Criophorus (lines 211-223, 535-544). Earliest Jesus representations mimicking Apollo is not in the chapter.

Ch3 inventory (lines 445-464) includes Josephus on Vespasian and Suetonius/Tacitus confirmations. The finding about Roman court historians reading Jewish prophecies as pointing to Caesar is the same evidence.
Luke's Emmaus as transvaluation of Romulus story belongs in chapter 4 or chapter 5.
Ch3 covers apologist comparisons (lines 47-50). The syncretistic integration of Adonis/Tammuz, Serapis, Hercules/Melcart as dying-rising gods translated into Greco-Roman terms is new evidence.

Ch3 discusses kingdom as political institution (lines 691-728). The observation that kingdom expectations confronted Rome's actual political dominance with huge temples -- and needed reframing -- is new contextual evidence.
Ch3 inventory (lines 465-474) and the opening section (lines 22-57) cover Son of God as standard Hellenistic title extensively.
The translation fable pattern with nearly 100 cases of deification is new quantitative evidence not cited in ch3.
Ch3 inventory (lines 620-633) includes Suetonius on Augustus's portents and divine conception. The finding restates this evidence.
Gethsemane as mimesis of Homer belongs in chapter 4.
Tomb narrative paralleling Iliad Book 24 belongs in chapter 4.

Ch3 discusses Father in Greek philosophical categories (lines 97-126). The specific transformation of Stoic divine governance (observable through nature) into Christian revealed textual authority is new evidence.
Ch3 inventory (lines 610-619) includes coins with theos sebastos kaiser and Render unto Caesar. Exact same evidence.
Ch3 inventory covers kingdom as political institution (lines 697-728) and lived allegiance (lines 857-870). The finding restates this argument.

Ch3 cites apologist comparisons (lines 47-50) but does not describe empty tomb narratives, eyewitness ascension, and deification as standardized Greco-Roman protocols. Miller's systematic framework with Peregrinus as example is new evidence.
Ch3 does not detail Romulus deification from Livy and Dionysius of Halicarnassus with multiple recensions. This is new evidence.

Ch3 covers Egyptian divine kingship extensively: Son of Ra (lines 465-474), Egyptian solar-royal culture (lines 211-223), Pater Noster parallels (lines 146-250). Egyptian king as simultaneously human and divine is covered.
Ch3 discusses early Christian art with solar symbols (lines 211-223, 272-287) but does not mention the ankh adopted as Coptic cross. This symbolic continuity is new evidence.
Ch3 inventory (lines 47-50) includes Theophilus citing Heracles and Asclepius. The finding about bodily immortalization extending beyond Jesus is covered.

Ch3 discusses Greek vs Jewish categories (lines 97-126). The specific distinction between apotheosis (becoming god/star) and angelification (becoming angel) as two distinct frameworks is not in the chapter.
Ch3 includes Augustus as divi filius (lines 22-57). Augustus becoming Son of God through adoption is the same evidence.
Ch3 does not compare Paul's spiritual resurrection with Luke/John's physical appearances. This contrast is new evidence.
Ch3 inventory (lines 87-96) covers the earliest Christian record being entirely Greek. The finding restates this.

Ch3 does not discuss Attis/Ashman's death-resurrection cult with its three-day Day of Blood/Day of Washing cycle. This specific ritual calendar paralleling Christian Easter timeline is both new evidence and new argument.
Ch3 does not discuss Sabazios carrying Dionysus in his bosom, or Clement of Alexandria admitting this is an inversion of the Mysteries of Sabazios. Both the argument and evidence are new.
Ch3 covers Augustus's titles and Suetonius (lines 22-57, 620-633). Augustus portrayed with Messianic language (savior, calmer of storms, healer) is the same evidence.
Revelation's Orphic deities belong in chapter 5 or chapter 6.
Ch3 mentions Serapis briefly (lines 581-587). The detailed comparison of Serapis to Jesus (healing, resurrection, apotheosis) is new evidence.
Ch3 inventory (lines 47-50) includes Justin Martyr and Theophilus comparing Jesus to Heracles and Dionysus. The finding restates covered evidence.
Ch3 cites individual examples (lines 47-50). The characterization of dying/rising god as ubiquitous trope with tons of examples across the entire Mediterranean is new framing.
Ch3 discusses divine conception through Plutarch/Luke (lines 476-485) and Son of God (lines 465-474). The pattern of supreme god plus mortal woman producing divine offspring is already covered.
Luke imitating Homer belongs in chapter 4 or chapter 5.
Ch3 discusses kingdom as political institution (lines 691-728). The observation that miracles of sign prophets always have a political edge is new evidence.

Ch3 discusses Father in Greek philosophical categories (lines 97-126) and immortal soul (lines 127-144). Paul's resurrection as spiritual pneuma reflecting Platonic dualism is new evidence connecting Paul's resurrection theology to Greek philosophy.
Ch3 discusses Christ as Helios in Christian art (lines 211-223). Helios in Jewish synagogues representing the coming Messiah is new evidence of solar imagery in Jewish contexts before Christianity.

Global Q&A rejects the mystery cult framing. The evidence (Mark 4:11-12 secret of the kingdom restricted to insiders) is relevant but the mystery cult initiation argument is new. The book would reframe this as operational security or chain of command per global Q&A, making the argument different.
Jesus using Greek theater vocabulary belongs in chapter 2.
Ch3 inventory (lines 445-464) includes Josephus on Vespasian and Suetonius/Tacitus. Titus as son of the god Vespasian is in the same evidence category.

Ch3 discusses Son of Man as one of four titles (lines 422-444). The Enoch Similitudes presenting Son of Man as a syncretic Greco-Jewish figure -- pre-existent, quasi-divine judge on throne of glory -- is not in the chapter's evidence.

Ch3 discusses royal titles and divine kingship. Enoch's exaltation with majestic robe, kingly crown, and title Lesser Yahweh is new evidence of pre-Christian royal investiture not cited in the chapter.
